coredata.dc:title: Detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline in water with a highly stable luminescent MOF
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, You
Yang, Qian
Zhang, Denan
Gan, Ning
Li, Qiuping
Cuan, Jing
coredata.dc:description: Antibiotic tetracycline (TC) is a sort of main contaminates in water, and of adverse effect on ecosystems and human health. The development of simple and efficient methods for both detection and removal of TC in water is highly desirable but remains challenging. Herein, a dual-functional platform for detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline (TC) is developed by a highly stable luminescent zirconium-based MOF (PCN-128Y). The detection is based on the efficient luminescence quenching of the PCN-128Y toward TC. Theoretical/experimental studies reveal that the luminescence quenching can be attributed to a combined effect of the strong absorption of TC at the excitation wavelength and the photo-induced electron transfer process from the ligand of PCN-128Y to TC. The strong cheating metal-ligand bonding between Zr6 nodes and TC through solvent-assisted ligand incorporation is suggested to mainly account for the high adsorption capability of PCN-128Y toward TC in water. The preconcentration of TC within the pores of PCN-128Y induced by the adsorption process makes TC contact with the framework more sufficient, thus significantly enhances the efficiency of TC sensing. This work is the first example demonstrating that MOF materials can integrate the functions of detection and removal of antibiotic TC in water, which highlights the opportunity of luminescent MOFs in the application of wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Antibiotic tetracycline detection
Adsorption of tetracycline
Metal-organic framework
Luminescent quenching mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the solubility partition coefficient of TC in PCN-128Y/water system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21701092 ), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. Q17B010002 , LY16B050003 and Q14B010001 , Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (No. 2016A610105 ), the research fund of NBU (No. ZX2016000466 ) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) View of the 3D framework of PCN-128Y featuring large hexagonal and triangular channel running through c axis. b) PXRD pattern of the as-obtained PCN-128Y and that simulated from the CIF file [42]. c) Photoluminescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) of aqueous suspensions (0.1 g/L) of PCN-128 and H4ETTC ligand. The intensity of the emission spectra of H4ETTC is magnified 20 folds. d) Emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) of PCN-128Y aqueous suspension in the absence and presence of TC (0.1 mM), inset: the pictures of PCN-128Y suspensions (with and without TC) under a 365 nm UV irradiation.

a) Emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) of PCN-128Y aqueous suspensions (0.1 g/L) in the presence of various concentrations of TC. b) The Stern−Volmer plot for the quenching of PCN-128Y by TC. c) Response of the luminescence intensity of PCN-128Y toward different antibiotics, I 0 and I denote the emission intensity of PCN-128 suspension in absence and presence of antibiotics, respectively.

a) Excitation and emission spectra of PCN-128Y and UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC. b) Emission spectra (λex = 420 nm) of PCN-128Y under different concentrations of TC (0–1 μM), inset: Stern−Volmer plot of TC. c) Calculated molecular orbital energy levels of H4ETTC ligand and the molecular orbital maps of the HOMO and LUMO. Wine and green represent the different phases of molecular wave functions for the HOMO and LUMO, and the isovalue is 0.02 au. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) UV–vis spectra of TC in water at different time during the adsorption for PCN-128Y as the adsorbent. The initial concentration of TC is 0.1 mM b) Adsorption isotherms of TNP and 4-NP in PCN-128Y (adsorption conditions: at 298 K, 50 mL of solution, 15 mg of MOFs, contact time of 4 h). The red solid line is the Langmuir fitting plot. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline in water with a highly stable luminescent MOF

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University of Technology, Ningbo, 315211, Zhejiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Superconducting & Electronic Materials, School of Mechanical, Materials and Mechatronics Engineering, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly stable luminescent MOF was demonstrated as a dual-functional platform for both detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline in water.

Antibiotic tetracycline (TC) is a sort of main contaminates in water, and of adverse effect on ecosystems and human health. The development of simple and efficient methods for both detection and removal of TC in water is highly desirable but remains challenging. Herein, a dual-functional platform for detection and removal of antibiotic tetracycline (TC) is developed by a highly stable luminescent zirconium-based MOF (PCN-128Y). The detection is based on the efficient luminescence quenching of the PCN-128Y toward TC. Theoretical/experimental studies reveal that the luminescence quenching can be attributed to a combined effect of the strong absorption of TC at the excitation wavelength and the photo-induced electron transfer process from the ligand of PCN-128Y to TC. The strong cheating metal-ligand bonding between Zr6 nodes and TC through solvent-assisted ligand incorporation is suggested to mainly account for the high adsorption capability of PCN-128Y toward TC in water. The preconcentration of TC within the pores of PCN-128Y induced by the adsorption process makes TC contact with the framework more sufficient, thus significantly enhances the efficiency of TC sensing. This work is the first example demonstrating that MOF materials can integrate the functions of detection and removal of antibiotic TC in water, which highlights the opportunity of luminescent MOFs in the application of wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: As an effective antibiotic, Tetracycline (TC) is extensively used for the treatment of bacterial infections in humans and animals due to its broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, low toxicity and cost, and favorable oral absorption. However, the abuse of TC in animal husbandry and aquaculture results in high level of residual TC in environment media, such as soil and water [1]. Residual TC has been regarded as an important organic contaminate in water [2]. It is hard to degrade and remains active form in environmental water, thus leading to adverse effect on ecosystems and human health. With increasing concern for water quality and public health, the development of efficient platforms for monitoring and removal of TC in water is highly desirable.

At present, the analysis of TC mainly rely on instruments such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatograph with tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) methods [3,4]. These techniques show high sensitivity and accuracy, but they are time-consuming and require expensive and complicated equipment, thus limiting their application in routine analysis. To complement instrumentation analysis, efforts have been made to develop electrochemical and luminescent sensors of TC. Compared with the electrochemical sensors, luminescent TC sensors gain more popularity owing to their ease of fabrication, simple operability, high sensitivity, and capability for in situ and non-invasive detection. To date, TC fluorescent sensors has been primarily developed based on carbon nanoparticles [5,6], semiconductor quantum dots labeled molecularly imprinted polymers [7–9], Eu3+ functionalized materials [10–12], polyelectrolytes [13,14] and graphene oxide hydrogel [15]. These checked materials are efficient in the determination of TC residues, but the majority of them can’t simultaneously function as adsorbent to remove TC residues from aqueous environment. Integrating both detection and removal functions of TC into one material is scarcely reported and remains challenging.

Luminescent MOFs featuring tunable intriguing structures, permanent porosity and intense fluorescence have triggered immense interest for potential application in fluorometric chemosensors in recent years [16–19]. The luminescent properties of MOFs generally have a strong response to their crystalline structures, coordination environment of metal centers, and their interactions with guest species (such as coordination and hydrogen bonds, π-π interactions), which provide solid rationale for fluorescent sensing. Hitherto luminescent MOFs have been intensively developed as chemosensors for various targets, such as metal cations [20–22], small molecules [23–27], gases [28], biomarkers [29–32], pH values [33,34], and temperatures [35–37]. In addition to the sensing applications, MOFs also exhibit good adsorption capability toward organic dyes and hazardous organics in water, thanks to their high porosity and specific interactions with guests [38–40]. Therefore, luminescent MOFs could be promising candidates for integrating the functions of detection and capture of various targets. Given the versatile structural and chemical tunability of luminescent MOFs, we reasoned that the detection and capture of TC could be accomplished by electron/energy transfer and specific host-guest interactions between TC and MOFs through judiciously selecting the component metal ions and organic ligands.

With all these in mind, we herein present an investigation of both detection and removal of TC residues in water based on a highly stable luminescent zirconium-based MOF (PCN-128Y). PCN-128Y is constructed by tetraphenylethylene (TPE)-based ligand (H4ETTC) and Zr6 clusters (Fig. S1). H4ETTC ligand behaves as a fluorophore, and its emission can be effectively quenched by TC, thus allowing the sensitive detection of TC. The Zr6 clusters with remaining coordination sites occupied by terminal OH/H2O facilitate the adsorption of antibiotic TC through solvent-assisted ligand incorporation (SALI) [41]. The adsorption process induces the preconcentration of TC in the pores of PCN-128Y, which makes TC contact with the framework more sufficient and therefore significantly enhances the efficiency of TC sensing. This is the first example utilizing a MOF material for integration of the sensitive detection and effective removal of TC in water.

All chemicals were commercially available and used without purification. Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), trifluoro acetic acid (TFA), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The antibiotics tetracycline(TC), kanamycin(KANA), erythromycin(ERY), penicillin(PEN), griseofulvin (GRI), streptomycin (STR), chloramphenicol (THI), nitro-furazone (NZF) were purchased from Adamas. The synthesis of ligand 4,4′,4”,4”'-(ethene-1,1,2,2-tetrayl) tetrakis ([1,1′-biphenyl]-4-carboxylic acid) (H4ETTC) and PCN-128Y was accomplished in direct analogy with the published procedures [42].

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were obtained on a D8 Advance X-Ray Powder Diffractometer (AXS, Brooke, Germany) using CuKα radiation with 40 mA and 40 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was carried out using a TM-3000 scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) was measured using a Netzsch STA 449C system at a heating rate of 5 K min−1 under nitrogen protection. Diffuse reflectance infrared spectra (DRIFTS) were performed on a Nicolet 7600 FTIR spectrometer equipped with an MCT detector. The spectra were measured in a KBr mixture under N2 purge. The photoluminescence spectra were examined by a RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). The absolute external luminescent quantum efficiency was determined employing an integrating sphere (150 mm diameter, BaSO4 coating) from an Edinburgh FLS920 phosphorimeter.

ZrCl4 (120.0 mg, 0.52 mmol), H4ETTC (60.0 mg, 0.072 mmol) and trifluoroacetic acid (0.8 mL) were ultrasonically mixed in a Pyrex tube containing 8.0 mL DMF. After heating the mixture at 120 °C for 48 h, a white solid product can be obtained. The white solid was transferred into a mixture of DMF and 1.0 mL 6 M HCl (VDMF:VHCl = 10:1), and then stirred at 100 °C in an oil bath for 12 h. After which, the solid was separated from the mixed dispersion by centrifugation, and then thoroughly washed with DMF (5 × 10 mL) and acetone (5 × 10 mL). Finally, the product was collected by centrifugation and dried at 70 °C for 6 h under vacuum. A yellow powder of PCN-128Y was obtained.

In a typical experimental setup, 3 mg of PCN-128Y powder was added to a tube containing 20 mL of deionized water, followed by ultrasound for 5 min to ensure the uniform dispersion. The PCN-128Y aqueous dispersion was keep stirring during the experiment to maintain its homogeneity. Then newly prepared aqueous tetracycline solution (0.01 mM) was incrementally added to the PCN-128Y suspension (1.9 mL). The mixtures were then used for luminescence measurements. The excitation wavelength of the luminescence measurements is 365 nm. The selectivity experiments were similarly conducted by adding other antibiotics into the aqueous suspensions of PCN-128Y, and then the emission spectra of the mixtures were recorded.

Before the adsorption experiments, the sample of PCN-128Y were activated by drying in a vacuum oven for 12 h at 120 °C. The activated PCN-128Y sample (1 mg) was added to the aqueous solution of TC (0.1 mM, 10 mL). The adsorption performance of PCN-128Y was characterized by monitoring the UV–vis spectra of the TC solution along with the soaking time at 25 °C. The adsorption isotherm of TC was obtained by the UV–vis measurements of the TC solutions (10−3–10 mM) being contacted with PCN-128Y for 4 h. The adsorbed amount of TC was determined by Eq. (1):
(1)
Q e
=
(
C 0
−
C e
)
V
M
where Qe (mg g−1) is the equilibrium adsorbed amount of TC; C0 and Ce (mg L−1) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of solution; V (L) is the volume of solution; and M (g) is the mass of PCN-128Y. The obtained adsorption isotherm can be well fitted by Langmuir mode (Eq. (2)):
(2)
C e
Q e
=
1
Q m
K
+
C e
Q m
where Qm (mg g−1) is the maximum monolayer adsorption capacity, and K (L g−1) is the Langmuir constant correlated to the free energy of adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the solubility partition coefficient of TC in PCN-128Y/water system
ce:section.ce:para:
The solubility partition coefficient of TC in PCN-128Y/water system is the ratio of equilibrium TC concentrations in the pores of PCN-128Y and water (CMOF/Ce). The equilibrium concentration of TC in water can be readily calculated with the help of UV–vis measurements. The concentration of TC in the channels of PCN-128Y can be determined by Eq. (3):
(3)
C
M O F
=
(
C 0
−
C e
)
V
s o l u t i o n
m
V
M O F
where C0 and Ce (mg L−1) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of solution, respectively; Vsolution (L) is the volume of TC aqueous solution; m (g) is mass of PCN-128Y; VMOF (cm3 g−1) is the calculated total pore volume of PCN-128Y. Herein, VMOF is determined as 1.32 cm3 g−1 based on the N2 adsorption-desorption measurement at 77 K.

The highly luminescent and stable Zr-MOF, PCN-128Y, was prepared by a solvothermal reaction of ZrCl4 and H4ETTC ligands in DMF and a followed activation in HCl solution. The 3D framework with a csq-a topology of PCN-128Y is formed by bridging Zr6 clusters and rectangular ETTC planar linkers (Fig. 1 a) [42]. It contains two types of 1D open channels—the hexagonal and triangular channel with respective diameters of 4.4 and 1.5 nm. PXRD pattern of the prepared PCN-128Y yellow solid matches well with that simulated from the single crystal structure (Fig. 1b) [39]. FSEM image indicates that the PCN-128Y product consists of microcrystals with the size in the range of 0.5–1.5 um (Fig. S2). PCN-128Y is highly stable, its crystalline framework retains intact after treatment for 2 days by water and acidic or weak basic conditions (Fig. S3). Thermogravimetric analysis shows that PCN-128Y is thermally stable up to 500 °C (Fig. S4). The exceptional chemical and thermal stability of PCN-128Y can be ascribed to the strong bonding between the Zr6 nodes and the carboxylates of ETTC linkers. The strong bonding force between Zr(IV) and carboxylate O atoms has been demonstrated in many carboxylate-based Zr-MOFs, which can be explained by the high charge density and bond polarization originated from the high oxide state of Zr (IV) [43].

The photoluminescence spectrum of PCN-128Y aqueous suspension was recorded at room temperature. The emission of PCN-128Y well inherits the feature of H4ETTC ligand (Fig. 1c). It displays a strong broad emission band centered at 538 nm, which can be ascribed to π-π* electron transition of H4ETTC ligands. However, the emission intensity of H4ETTC is much lower than that of PCN-128Y. In addition, PCN-128Y exhibits a higher luminescence quantum yield (φ) of 85.2% in comparison with that of H4ETTC (22.3%). We reasoned that the immobilization of H4ETTC in the framework structure will suppress the rotation and vibration of benzene rings, thus reducing the nonradiative transitions and giving rise to a higher φ. In order to ascertain the fluorescence stability of PCN-128Y in water, the time and pH-dependent emission spectra of PCN-128Y in aqueous solutions were conducted. As shown in Fig. S5 and 6, the water treatment and pH variation have a very limited influence on the emission intensity of PCN-128Y. The outstanding fluorescence stability of PCN-128Y aqueous suspension is mainly originated from the exceptional chemical stability of PCN-128Y in water and acidic or weak basic conditions. The good water tolerance together with the high luminescence performance implies that PCN-128Y can be utilized as an excellent candidate for fluorometric sensing in aqueous systems.

To examine the feasibility of antibiotic TC sensing for PCN-128Y, the luminescence response toward TC was collected. As shown in Fig. 1d, concentrated TC (0.1 mM) produces a significant luminescence quenching (∼90%) for PCN-128Y. Time-dependent emission spectra of PCN-128Y in the presence of TC indicate that the luminescent response rate of PCN-128Y toward TC is very fast (Fig. S7). The emission of PCN-128Y is almost fully quenched upon contacting with TC only for 1 min. In addition, the luminescence quenching of PCN-128Y caused by TC can be readily observed by naked eye under a laboratorial 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1d). These results demonstrate that PCN-128Y has the ability of in-situ visualizing the TC concentration variation instantly and straightforwardly, thus can be potentially fabricated as TC test strip for routine analysis.

For better understanding the luminescence response of PCN-128Y to TC, the luminescence titration upon addition of TC to PCN-128Y suspension was conducted. Specifically, TC was added incrementally to the aqueous suspension of PCN-128Y, increasing the TC concentration from 0 to 1 μM, and the emission spectra were measured (Fig. 2 a). The emission intensity of PCN-128Y suspension decreases progressively with the increase of TC concentration. The luminescent quenching effect can be quantitatively rationalized by the Stern-Volmer equation
(4)
I 0/I = 1 + K sv × [Q]
where K sv is the quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of the analyte, I 0 and I are the luminescence intensities in the absence and presence of the analyte. Fig. 2b plots the K sv curve of PCN-128Y with TC. The K sv value is determined as 9.84 × 105 M−1, which is higher than that of the previously reported fluorometric sensors of TC [7,8]. The large K sv value reveals the strong quenching effect of TC on the luminescence of PCN-128Y, as well as the high sensitivity of PCN-128Y for TC detection. Calculation of the limit of detection (LOD) based on the Ksv values and the standard deviations gave a value of 30 nM.

The high sensitivity and low LOD are supposed to be related with the preconcentration of TC in the channels of PCN-128Y. This preconcentration effect can be evaluated by the solubility partition coefficient of TC in water/PCN-128Y system, which is defined as the ratio of equilibrium TC concentrations within PCN-128Y and in water (CMOF/CSolution). Based on the UV–vis absorption data (Fig. S8), the solubility partition coefficient of TC in PCN-128Y/water system is calculated as 1.63 × 104 (calculation method see the experimental section). This large solubility partition coefficient validates the preconcentration effect of TC within PCN-128Y. Besides, the luminescence of PCN-128Y is mainly originated from the H4ETTC ligand, it thus enables us to estimate the preconcentration effect by comparing the luminescence quenching constants of PCN-128 with that of H4ETTC ligand toward TC. The luminescence response of H4ETTC ligand toward TC was studied under the same condition with PCN-128Y (Fig. S9). The resulted K sv value (6.6 × 104 M−1) is far smaller than that of PCN-128Y (9.84 × 105 M−1), which confirms that the high TC detection ability of PCN-128Y is closely correlated with the preconcentration of TC. The preconcentration of TC within PCN-128Y is resulted from the adsorption process of TC, which will be discussed in the sections below.

To establish the performance of selectivity of PCN-128Y toward TC, the luminescence changes of PCN-128Y in the presence of other antibiotics were investigated. As can be seen from Fig. 2c, PCN-128Y only shows slight luminescence responses to the antibiotics such as penicillin (PEN), griseofulvin (GRI), erythromycin (ERY), streptomycin (STR), chloramphenicol (THI), kanamycin (KANA), and nitro-furazone (NZF). While upon the introduction of TC to mixtures of PCN-128Y and other antibiotics, the emission of PCN-128Y was significantly quenched (Fig. S10). These results demonstrate the high selectivity and anti-interference ability of PCN-128Y for TC detection.

We next investigate the luminescence quenching mechanism of PCN-128Y towards TC. The UV–vis absorption of TC (Fig. 3 a) is basically not overlapped with the emission of PCN-128Y, thus excluding the energy transfer from PCN-128Y to TC. However, the strong absorption of TC at excitation wavelength (365 nm) might effectively suppress the excitation energy absorption of PCN-128Y, which leads to low populated emissive state of PCN-128Y and therefore significant quenching of fluorescence. To verify whether the strong absorption of TC at the excitation wavelength is responsible for the observed luminescence quenching, the luminescence response of PCN-128Y to TC was examined under an excitation of 420 nm (which is not in the range of TC absorption). Upon excitation at 420 nm, luminescence quenching of PCN-128Y also occurred with the addition of TC (Fig. 3b). Nevertheless, the luminescence quenching (K sv = 2.59 × 105 M−1) is less significant than that upon 365 nm excitation (K sv = 9.84 × 105 M−1). These results reveal that the strong absorption of TC at excitation wavelength only partially contributes to the luminescence quenching of PCN-128Y.

Another possible mechanism for the luminescence quenching is photoinduced electron transfer (PET). Since the luminescence of PCN-128Y is originated from H4ETTC ligand, the PET process between H4ETTC ligand and TC might quench the luminescence of PCN-128Y. Specifically, electrons in the molecular orbital (HOMO) of H4ETTC are excited to its lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) followed by transfer to the LUMO of TC. To examine the possibility of this process, we calculated the relative energies of HOMOs and LUMOs of H4ETTC and TC by the density functional theory (DFT) method at the B3LYP/6-31G* level, as illustrated in Fig. 3c. The resulted energy band gap between HOMOs and LUMOs of H4ETTC (2.49 eV) is in reasonable agreement with that calculated from the solid state diffuse reflectance spectrum (2.30 eV) [42]. The LUMO energy level of H4ETTC (−3.06 eV) is higher than that of TC (−4.53 eV) [14], which supports the PET from H4ETTC to TC. Therefore, it is concluded that both the PET process and the strong absorption of TC at excitation wavelength (365 nm) contribute to the luminescence quenching of PCN-128Y.

In addition to detection, the removal of residual TC from water systems is also of great significance in water treatment. We hence performed the adsorption studies of PCN-128Y for TC. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of TC aqueous solution (0.1 mM) in the presence of PCN-128Y (0.1 g/L). It is observed that PCN-128Y possesses high uptake capability for TC, the adsorption rate of TC solution (0.1 mM) is up to 56% in a short time (30 min). To evaluate and compare the adsorption activity of PCN-128Y and other porous materials (zeolite 4A, SBA-15, and MCM-41) for TC, the similar adsorption experiments of TC for zeolite 4A, SBA-15, and MCM-41 were conducted (Fig. S11). These porous materials show trace adsorption toward TC, which highlights the superior adsorption capability of PCN-128Y for TC. Besides, the adsorption isotherm of TC in PCN-128Y was investigated at 25 °C (Fig. 4b). The equilibrium data can be well fitted by the Langmuir model, revealing the adsorption occurs at the specific homogeneous sites. The maximum adsorption amount (Qm) of TC in PCN-128Y is 423 mg g−1, which is considerably high in comparison with those in other TC adsorbing materials reported so far (Table S1).

Considering that the connectivity of PCN-128Y generates Zr6 nodes with terminal OH/H2O groups [42], and the liable OH/H2O on Zr6 nodes can be displaced by organic molecules via solvent-assisted ligand incorporation (SALI) [41,44], we reasoned that SALI process play a key role in the high TC adsorption capacity of PCN-128Y (Fig. S12). The evidence of SALI is provided by the diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) studies (Fig. S13). A peak at 3662 cm−1 is observed for PCN-128Y, which can be assigned to terminal OH group. While this peak of PCN-128 disappears upon the adsorption of TC, suggesting that the terminal OH sites are occupied by the adsorbed TC. The peak appears at 3620 cm−1 belongs to the remaining OH group of TC. In addition, the adsorbed TC within PCN-128Y can hardly be removed. Upon washing three times with ethanol through stirring overnight at room temperature, the TC adsorbed PCN-128Y showed little recovery in emission intensity (Fig. S14). This result supports that the majority of adsorbed TC form strong cheating metal-ligand bond with Zr6 nodes through the SALI process.

In summary, we demonstrate that a robust luminescent zirconium-based MOF, PCN-128Y, can serve as an excellent platform for both detection and removal of antibiotic TC in water. The detection of TC is based on the sensitive luminescence quenching of PCN-128Y toward TC. Theoretical/experimental results reveal that the luminescence quenching can be attributed to a combined effect of the strong absorption of TC at the excitation wavelength and the PET process from the ligand of PCN-128Y to TC. In addition, PCN-128Y exhibits good adsorption capability toward TC in water, which is mainly thanks to the strong cheating metal-ligand bonding between Zr6 nodes and TC through a SALI process. The preconcentration of TC in the pores of PCN-128Y derived from the adsorption process significantly enhances the TC sensing performance of PCN-128Y. Present study highlights the opportunities of luminescent MOF materials to explore the applications of detection and removal of antibiotic contaminations in water.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21701092), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. Q17B010002, LY16B050003 and Q14B010001, Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (No. 2016A610105), the research fund of NBU (No. ZX2016000466) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.01.218.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Genetics and Biochemistry of Antibiotic Production
Antibiotics and antibiotic resistance in water environments
Multi-residue determination of veterinary drugs in milk by ultra-high-pressure liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Validation of a high-performance liquid chromatography method for the determination of oxytetracycline tetracycline, chlortetracycline and doxycycline in bovine milk and muscle
Imaging two targets in live cells based on rational design of lanthanide organic structure appended carbon dots
One-pot synthesis of high fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their applications as probes for detection of tetracyclines
Direct and highly selective drug optosensing in real, undiluted biological samples with quantum-dot-labeled hydrophilic molecularly imprinted polymer microparticles
Fluorescence probe based on hybrid mesoporous silica/quantum dot/molecularly imprinted polymer for detection of tetracycline
Comparative syntheses of tetracycline-imprinted polymeric silicate and acrylate on CdTe quantum dots as fluorescent sensors
Europium functionalized ratiometric fluorescent transducer silicon nanoparticles based on FRET for the highly sensitive detection of tetracycline
Silver nanoparticle enhanced fluorescence of europium (III) for detection of tetracycline in milk
Determination of tetracycline in milk by using nucleotide/lanthanide coordination polymer-based ternary complex
Hybrid silica-polyelectrolyte films as optical sensing materials for tetracycline antibiotics
Conjugated polyelectrolyte based sensitive detection and removal of antibiotics tetracycline from water
A versatile fluorescent biosensor based on target-responsive graphene oxide hydrogel for antibiotic detection
Metal-organic frameworks: functional luminescent and photonic materials for sensing applications
Lanthanide metal-organic frameworks for luminescent sensing and light-emitting applications
Luminescent metal-organic frameworks for chemical sensing and explosive detection
An Eu3+ post-functionalized nanosized metal-organic framework for cation exchange-based Fe3+-sensing in an aqueous environment
A luminescent metal-organic framework with lewis basic pyridyl sites for the sensing of metal ions
Intelligent molecular searcher from logic computing network based on Eu(III) functionalized UMOFs for environmental monitoring
A responsive MOF nanocomposite for decoding volatile organic compounds
Controlled lanthanide-organic framework nanospheres as reversible and sensitive luminescent sensors for practical applications
Highly sensitive detection of dipicolinic acid with a water-dispersible terbium-metal organic framework
Copper (II)-mediated fluorescence of lanthanide coordination polymers doped with carbon dots for ratiometric detection of hydrogen sulfide
A double-stimuli-responsive fluorescent center for monitoring of food spoilage based on dye covalently modified EuMOFs: from sensory hydrogels to logic devices
Photoluminescent metal-organic frameworks for gas sensing
Determination of urinary 1-Hydroxypyrene for biomonitoring of human exposure to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons carcinogens by a lanthanide-functionalized metal-organic framework sensor
A mixed-crystal lanthanide zeolite-like metal-organic framework as a fluorescent indicator for lysophosphatidic acid, a cancer biomarker
Rapid and facile ratiometric detection of an anthrax biomarker by regulating energy transfer process in bio-metal-organic framework
Eu3+ functionalized Sc-MOFs: turn-on fluorescent switch for ppb-level biomarker of plastic pollutant polystyrene in serum and urine and on-site detection by smartphone
A visually detectable pH responsive zirconium metal-organic framework
An exceptionally stable, porphyrinic Zr metal-organic framework exhibiting pH-dependent fluorescence
Visible-light excited luminescent thermometer based on single lanthanide organic frameworks
Postsynthetic lanthanide functionalization of nanosized metal-organic frameworks for highly sensitive ratiometric luminescent thermometry
A luminescent mixed-lanthanide metal-organic framework thermometer
Removal of hazardous organics from water using metal-organic frameworks (MOFs): plausible mechanisms for selective adsorptions
Rational construction of defects in a metal-organic framework for highly efficient adsorption and separation of dyes
A new microporous anionic metal-organic framework as a platform for highly selective adsorption and separation of organic dyes
Perfluoroalkane functionalization of NU-1000 via solvent-assisted ligand incorporation: synthesis and CO2 adsorption studies
Piezofluorochromic metal-organic framework: a microscissor lift
Zr-based metal-organic frameworks: design, synthesis, structure, and applications
Postsynthetic tuning of metal-organic frameworks for targeted applications

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: You Zhou received his PhD degree in 2016 from the department of chemistry, Tongji University. Now he is working at the school of materials science and chemical engineering, Ningbo University. His current interests involve chemical sensors and luminescent materials.

Qian Yang obtained her BS degree at Xiangtan University in 2016. She is currently a master candidate at Ningbo University. Her research interest mainly focus on metal-organic frameworks based chemical sensors.
Denan Zhang is currently a master candidate at the school of materials science and chemical engineering, Ningbo University. His research interest is luminescent sensors.
Ning Gan received his PhD degree in analytic chemistry from Nanjing University in 2002. Now he is a professor of Ningbo University. His research areas include biochips and biosensors.
Qiuping Li received his PhD degree in 2013 from Tongji University. Now he is lecturer of Ningbo University of Technology. He is currently engaged in optical materials.
Jing Cuan received her master degree from Tongji University in 2014. Now she is PhD candidate at University of Wollongong.
